ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Raan e Dum; FOODON_03302051 (lamb dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (lamb meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for dum-braised whole lamb leg exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Raan e Dum is approximately 300 g, representing a generous portion of slow-cooked whole leg of lamb with accompanying spiced gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton (Goat/Lamb), cooked, composite — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for cooked mutton/lamb (bone-free, composite cut) used as the primary nutrient basis. Raan e Dum is a whole leg of lamb slow-cooked with yoghurt, spices, and ghee; the marinade and cooking fat contribute additional energy and fat. Values adjusted upward for fat (~+3 g/100 g) and sodium (~+120 mg/100 g) to account for the spiced yoghurt marinade and added ghee, consistent with published recipe analyses for dum-cooked lamb preparations. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, NDB 17174, Lamb leg roasted) used as a cross-reference for protein and energy plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Raan e Dum. GI estimated at 30 based on the analogous category of cooked meat dishes with minimal carbohydrate content. Meat-based dishes with low carbohydrate content (≤5 g/100 g) consistently show GI ≤ 35 in the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables and in Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed work. The small carbohydrate contribution comes from yoghurt marinade and spices; GL per 300 g serving = (30 × 7.5 g carb) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spiced marinade and added salt (~320 mg/100 g; ~960 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 960 mg sodium, representing 42% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~2.5 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~30) minimise postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raan e Dum is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as naan or rice, as its low carbohydrate content and estimated GI of 30 produce a glycemic load of approximately 2.3 per standard serving, which is clinically negligible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~5.2 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~85 mg/100 g) from lamb and ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should moderate intake of Raan e Dum to occasional consumption, as a 300 g serving provides approximately 15.6 g saturated fat (78% of the AHA daily limit of 20 g) and 255 mg dietary cholesterol; requesting preparation with minimal ghee can reduce saturated fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raan e Dum (also written Raan-e-Dum) is a classic Mughlai North Indian dish consisting of a whole leg of lamb (raan) that is deeply marinated in a spiced yoghurt mixture containing ginger, garlic, cumin, coriander, garam masala, turmeric, chilli, saffron, and lemon juice, then slow-cooked using the dum technique — a method of sealing the pot with dough or a tight lid to trap steam and allow the meat to braise in its own juices at low heat for several hours. The result is exceptionally tender, fall-off-the-bone lamb with a rich, aromatic, mildly spiced crust. At some restaurants, including Kinara at Boat Quay in Singapore, the dish is finished in a tandoor oven to impart a characteristic smoky char on the exterior while retaining moisture within. Nutritionally, Raan e Dum is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with very low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The fat profile includes both saturated fat from lamb and ghee and beneficial monounsaturated fats. Sodium content is moderate to high due to the spiced marinade. The dish has a low glycemic index (~30) and is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when served without high-GI accompaniments. It is a culturally significant celebratory dish in Mughlai and North Indian cuisine, often served at weddings and special occasions, and is a recognised restaurant speciality in Indian fine-dining establishments across Singapore.

ingredient_synonyms.dum: slow steam-cooking technique with sealed pot
ingredient_synonyms.garam masala: Indian spice blend (cloves, cardamom, cinnamon, black pepper, cumin, coriander)

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Raan e Dum as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked mutton/lamb composite data with adjustments for the spiced yoghurt marinade and added ghee, cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy lamb leg roasted values. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (cooked lamb leg) is well-characterised in approved sources and the adaptations are conservative and documented.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods source for cooked mutton/lamb. 2. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 3. Nutrient values represent the edible portion of the cooked dish per 100 g, bone-free. 4. The restaurant version at Kinara - Boat Quay (field survey data, GD004088) is described as a tandoor-baked lamb leg; the dum and tandoor finishing steps are both reflected in the preparation method classification. 5. Halal status is flagged as likely true given the Indian Muslim/Mughlai culinary tradition of this dish and the restaurant context, but has not been independently verified for this specific restaurant. 6. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Raan e Dum was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the processing date. 7. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 7.5) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3.
